title: Lake Warming Hiatus: Physical Mechanisms
text: Understanding the physical mechanisms behind hiatus-period lake temperature response is essential — changepoint detection tells us when and how much, but not why.

title: Lake Warming Hiatus: Physical Mechanisms
text:
Surface energy balance framework
Lake surface temperature is governed by (O’Reilly et al. 2015):
\[\Delta T_{\text{surface}} \propto Q^* - Q_H - Q_E - \Delta Q_S\]
where \(Q^*\) = net radiation, \(Q_H\) = sensible heat, \(Q_E\) = latent heat (evaporation), \(\Delta Q_S\) = heat storage change. Any climate factor altering these four fluxes affects lake warming rate.

title: Lake Warming Hiatus: Physical Mechanisms
text:
Mechanisms

1. Air temperature conduction (primary driver)
Lake SST is highly coupled to air temperature (O’Reilly et al. 2015: global lake summer SST warming at ~0.34 °C/decade). During the hiatus (1998–2012), global mean air warming slowed to ~0.05 °C/decade (long-term ~0.18 °C/decade). 83% of 155 lakes slowed warming accordingly (Winslow et al. 2018).
But conduction is not 1:1 — lakes have their own buffering and amplification.


2. Evaporative cooling feedback ⭐⭐⭐
Zhou et al. (2023, Nature Communications) found: “the rate of lake heating slows as air warms”
Mechanism chain:

Air warms → lake-air vapour pressure deficit increases
Evaporation intensifies (Clausius-Clapeyron: ~7% per °C)
Evaporation consumes latent heat (~2.45 MJ/kg) → surface cooling
Evaporative cooling partially offsets radiative heating → net warming slows

Implication for hiatus: if hiatus-era lakes were already warm, evaporative cooling acts as a negative feedback amplifying the hiatus signal.


3. Solar dimming/brightening

1950s–1980s: global dimming (aerosols reduce surface shortwave)
1980s–2000s: global brightening (clean air legislation)
Post-2000s: regional divergence (China brightening, India dimming)

During hiatus, partial dimming return (post-1998 volcanic activity, China coal aerosols) reduced \(Q^*\) → weakened lake surface heating.


4. Wind stilling
Global terrestrial near-surface wind speed has declined ~0.1 m/s/decade since 1980s.
Effects on lakes:

Weakened turbulent mixing → shallower thermocline
More heat concentrated at surface → apparent surface warming may increase
But reduced wind also reduces evaporation → net effect is ambiguous

Woolway et al. (2017): wind stilling extends stratification period and increases surface warming in UK lakes.


5. Ice cover feedback
For seasonally frozen lakes (Magnuson et al. 2000):

Reduced winter/spring ice → earlier ice-off
Low-albedo water surface exposed → more solar absorption
Earlier heating → higher summer temperatures
Later freeze-up → shorter ice season → positive feedback loop

If hiatus period saw continued ice reduction (winter temperatures did not stagnate), ice feedback may partially offset hiatus cooling.


6. Large-scale teleconnections








Index
Key phase during hiatus
Lake response




PDO
Negative (La Niña-like) from 1998
Cooler tropical Pacific → lower global temps


AMO
Positive from mid-1990s
Warmer North Atlantic → offsets hiatus in N. Atlantic lakes


ENSO
La Niña dominant 1998–2012
Cooler global temps, esp. tropics/subtropics


NAO
Variable
Dominant control on European winter lake temperature



CP years (e.g. 1998) align with PDO phase transitions — detected changepoints may capture natural variability phase shifts, not just “global warming hiatus”.


7. Lake morphometry modulation








Factor
Effect
Mechanism




Depth
Deep lakes warm slower
Greater thermal inertia


Area
Large lakes: more evaporation
Longer fetch → stronger latent heat loss


Transparency
Clear lakes warm faster
Shortwave penetrates deeper


Altitude
High-altitude lakes more sensitive
Thinner atmosphere → stronger LW exchange


Trophic state
Eutrophic: complex response
Algae absorb shortwave → surface heating



Winslow et al. (2018): hiatus response showed no significant clustering by lake attributes — climate signal is strong enough to override morphometric differences.

title: Lake Warming Hiatus: Physical Mechanisms
text:
Integrated framework
          Global radiative forcing (CO₂ + aerosols)
                     │
      ┌──────────────┼──────────────┐
      ▼              ▼              ▼
  Atmospheric      Surface        Cloud/radiation
  circulation      air temp       changes
 (PDO/NAO/ENSO)      │              │
      │              │              │
      ▼              ▼              ▼
┌──────────── Lake surface energy balance ────────────┐
│  Q* (radiation)  Q_H (sensible)  Q_E (latent)  ΔQ_S │
└──────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┘
                       │
            ┌──────────┼──────────┐
            ▼          ▼          ▼
        Acceleration  Steady    Deceleration

title: Lake Warming Hiatus: Physical Mechanisms
text:
Sensitivity analysis implications
Based on the mechanism framework:

Evaporation: ERA5 evaporation trends → test if enhanced evaporation correlates with more significant changepoints
Wind: ERA5 u10/v10 → wind trends vs hiatus response
Radiation: ERA5 surface downward shortwave → radiation changes vs CP spatial consistency
PDO/AMO: test CP significance after removing natural variability
Ice cover: GLAST/ERA5 ice data → do frozen lakes differ?

title: xie2017: What Caused the Global Surface Warming Hiatus of 1998–2013?
text:
Notes
Global Surface Warming Hiatus(GSWH) is 1998-2013. Approx 0.027 - 0.086 degree Celsius per decade, which is much smaller than the 0.2 degree Celsius per decade during 1970s - 1990s.
crumbs: xie2017: What Caused the Global Surface Warming Hiatus of 1998–2013?

title: rodionov2004: A Sequential Algorithm for Testing Climate Regime Shifts
text: An algorithm to detect climate regime shifts. Classic work, widely cited.
crumbs: rodionov2004: A Sequential Algorithm for Testing Climate Regime Shifts

title: rodionov2004: A Sequential Algorithm for Testing Climate Regime Shifts
text:
Overview
No gonna describe it in great detail.

First, it’s sequential, meaning it processes the time series in order, and different start points can lead to different results.
Second, it processes new data points as they come in. Every new data is checked immediately. so it’s kinda near-real-time.
Third, since it deals the data points in a specific window, abnormal values won’t have a decisive influence on the results.

Algo accepts two parameters: cut-off length \(l\) and significance level \(p\).
crumbs: rodionov2004: A Sequential Algorithm for Testing Climate Regime Shifts

title: rodionov2004: A Sequential Algorithm for Testing Climate Regime Shifts
text:
Breif Intro

Calculate \(diff\) according to the series and \(l\): \[
  diff = t \sqrt{2 \sigma_l^2 / l} \tag{1}
\] where \(t\) is the critical value of the Student’s t distribution with \(2l-2\) degrees of freedom at the significance level \(p\) (two-tailed), and \(\sigma_l^2\) is the average variance of the series in running windows of length \(l\).
Calculate the \(\overline{x}_{Rk}\) where \(Ri\) is the \(k\)-th regime. Initially, \(R1\) is the first \(l\) data points.
For each new data point \(x_i\) where \(i\) starts from \(l+1\):

If \(|\overline{x}_{Rk} - x_i| > diff\), then a new possible regime is detected. The possible new regime \(R_{k+1}\) starts from \(x_i\). Move to 5.
Otherwise, \(x_i\) is not significantly different from the current regime, meaning the current regime continues, and we should update \(\overline{x}_{Rk}\) by including \(x_i\). Move back to 3 to check the next data point.

For the new possible regime \(R_{k+1}\) which starts from \(i\), calculate the \(RSI_{i,i}\) to \(RSI_{i+l-1,i}\). \(RSI\) is calculated as: \[
  RSI_{i,j} = \sum_{i=j}^{j+m} \frac{x_i^*} {l \sigma_l}, m=0,1,\dots,l-1 \tag{2}
\] where \(x_i^* = x_i - \overline{x}_{Rk} - diff\) if the shift is up, \(x_i^* = \overline{x}_{Rk} - x_i - diff\) if the shift is down.

If any of them is negetive, then the possible new regime is rejected. Update the current regime \(R_k\) by including \(x_i\) and move back to 3 to check the next data point.
Otherwise, all the \(RSI\)s are positive, then the possible new regime is accepted. New regime \(R_{k+1}\) starts from \(x_i\) and move back to 3 to check the next data point.
crumbs: rodionov2004: A Sequential Algorithm for Testing Climate Regime Shifts

title: rodionov2004: A Sequential Algorithm for Testing Climate Regime Shifts
text:
Little notes
Note that once a new regime is detected, the next to-be-checked data point is the next point of the regime shift point, no plus \(l\). For instance, if the 1950 is detected as a regine shift, meaning \(RSI_{1950,1950}\) to \(RSI_{1959,1950}\) are all positive, then the next to-be-checked data point is still 1951, not 1960. This simply means that a regime is not necessarily \(l\) long, it can be shorter, and the next regime can start at any point after the shift point.
However, the shift point is usually unlikely to exist in the first \(l\) data points after previous shift point, because points in \(l\) length window are not likely to be significantly different from the current regime.
crumbs: rodionov2004: A Sequential Algorithm for Testing Climate Regime Shifts

title: xiao2023: A Regime Shift in North Pacific Annual Mean Sea Surface Temperature in 2013/14
text:
Notes
Sea Surface Temperature (SST) in the North Pacific (NP) has experienced a regime shift in 2013/14, with a significant increase in the annual mean SST.
Not related to PDO and IPO, but Victoria Mode, which is defined as the second EOF mode of the annual mean SST in the NP, and is characterized by a horseshoe pattern of SST anomalies.
Says that might indicate hiatus is over.
crumbs: xiao2023: A Regime Shift in North Pacific Annual Mean Sea Surface Temperature in 2013/14

title: yan2016: The Global Warming Hiatus: Slowdown or Redistribution?
text:
This is a review
Differend researches define the “hiatus” differently. For instance, IPCC AR5 claimed hiatus should be 1998-2012, comparing with 1951-2012.
Global surface mean temperature increased slowly from 1951-1970. Then there was a rapid in mid-1970s, which was probably due to positive phase of PDO and AMO.
If we are talking about “global warming hiatus”, it should refer to a pause in the rate of increase of heat energy in the Earth’s system, not just rate of increase in surface temperature.

Arguably, the most appropriate single variable in the Earth’s system that can be used to monitor global warming is ocean heat content (OHC), integrated from the surface to the bottom of the ocean.
crumbs: yan2016: The Global Warming Hiatus: Slowdown or Redistribution?

title: Hola!
text:
This site contains my research explorations, drafts, and progress logs.
These materials were previously hosted on my main personal website. However, to separate my research content from my personal content, all research and academic exploration materials are now hosted on this site.
Check all explorations, including the finished, cancelled, not started or in-progress ones. Each exploration has its own draft, prose, and log.
I list frequently discussed metrics (methods, algorithms, parameters, decision records), trying not to make anyone confused.
Notes collect literature notes, mechanism summaries, and useful excerpts for writing.

To learn more about me, visit froq.me.



 Back to top

title: Whether the Hiatus Actually Exists
text: I previously believed that the “global warming hiatus” referred to the period from 1998 to 2012. However, around 2016, many studies questioned this definition, with some even arguing that no such hiatus actually occurred.

title: Whether the Hiatus Actually Exists
text:
The argue
The main documents related to this dispute are listed below:

Medhaug et al. (2017), Nature(Research Analysis): The so-called “hiatus” is a phenomenon resulting from the combined effects of definitions, observational coverage, internal natural variations, and external forcing. It does not mean that long-term human-induced warming has stopped.
Lewandowsky et al. (2015), Scientific Reports: There are no recognizable hiatuses or pauses in the data. What’s observed is simply the normal variability associated with modern warming. When examined from various perspectives, the trend over the past 17 years remains significant across all time periods. Short-term trends, however, are often not significant due to insufficient statistical power resulting from small sample sizes. These short-term fluctuations don’t represent actual pauses in warming.
Fyfe et al. (2016), Nature Climate Change: There has been a temporary slowdown, not a statistical illusion. This phenomenon was indeed observed between 2001 and 2014. However, it doesn’t represent a change in the long-term trend. It’s merely a short-term fluctuation within the overall long-term warming trend.

92,245 lakes in GlAST, 36% of which are in the Arctic (lowering the global mean).
There is a Hylak_id field in GlAST, corresponding to HydroLAKES v1.0, which can be used for attribute filtering.



 Back to top

3-year is too short. Lots of mutation located at 1980, kind of “edge effect”. (Honestly what should I call it?)
Use Mann-Kendall to detect mutation of trend.



 Back to top

Got 15th of each month ESA CCI water temperature data (global raster is too complete and too large).
Comparing 4 datasets: similar trend, but GLAST is lower (contains many polar lakes).



 Back to top

Thinking about using ERA5-Land to get lake surface temperature: MODIS LST + ERA5-Land vars to do ML.
ERA5-Land has a var called lake cover, can be used to filter the “pure” water pixel.
In this plan, we need to filter the area of the lakes, at least 1 ERA5-Land pixel, which is 0.1° grid.



 Back to top

Feedback from advisor: the paper is “rather flat”, needs to refine and highlight novel points.
But recent 2 weeks are spent in Dongshan station.



 Back to top

On the business trip again, limited progress.
STL can only run on my Mac mini (~1 hour/run). So it has to be moved back home for long-term internet connection.
Framework is temporarily determined, will continue to advance after returning.



 Back to top

Adjustment: The key point is not extract the water boundary precisely, but to get lake surface temperature efficiently. Consider the lake as a uniform water unit, so we just need to make sure the pixel is water pixel.




 Back to top

title: Latest Log
text:
Wait for redirection.






 Back to top

First-difference. But may increase the noise. How about do a 3-year smooth?



 Back to top

Use Yang’s data to do clustering analysis. Use the similarity of mutational features (Δslope, Δmean, Δvar) to do clustering.
Results of significance tests are all not significant, so maybe remove the significance constraint.
Maybe first PCA?



 Back to top

Hiatus frame -> 3-segment structure identification
Apply mutation detection to each lake temperature time series: 1998±4 and 2012±4 windows, find a breakpoint in each, cut into pre/mid/post segments.
Confirmed high heterogeneity in lake responses to hiatus, some lakes show no obvious response



 Back to top

The “hiatus” concept is controversial: Medhaug et al. (2017) (apparent), Lewandowsky et al. (2015) (nonexistent), Fyfe et al. (2016) (real but short-term modulation).
So, 1998-2012 is not a good reference period for global warming acceleration.




 Back to topReferences

Fyfe, John C., Gerald A. Meehl, Matthew H. England, et al. 2016. “Making Sense of the Early-2000s Warming Slowdown.” Nature Climate Change 6 (3): 224–28. https://doi.org/10.1038/nclimate2938.


Lewandowsky, Stephan, James S. Risbey, and Naomi Oreskes. 2015. “On the Definition and Identifiability of the Alleged ‘Hiatus’ in Global Warming.” Scientific Reports 5 (1): 16784. https://doi.org/10.1038/srep16784.


Medhaug, Iselin, Martin B. Stolpe, Erich M. Fischer, and Reto Knutti. 2017. “Reconciling Controversies about the ‘Global Warming Hiatus’.” Nature 545 (7652): 41–47. https://doi.org/10.1038/nature22315.

title: Descriptive
text:
Lake density map








Figure 1: Lake density map with 1° × 1° grid cells.

title: Descriptive
text:
Long-term warming && acceleration
Table 1 (a) and Table 1 (b) shows the statistics of warming. Compared with the raw annual mean temperature series, the STL trend component is smoother and more stable, so it identifies warming in more lakes (91,802 / 92,245, 99.5%, versus 85,569 / 92,245, 92.8% for the raw series). It also has a smaller standard deviation and a more concentrated quartile range, although its mean is lower (0.573 versus 0.881 for the raw series).


Use Sen’s slope to determine whether a lake is warming and how is the trend like.
Though almost all the lakes are warming, the warming is not uniform. Acceleration of 51,014 (55.3%) lakes is positive. Standard deviation and quartile range are also large, indicating that the acceleration varies widely across lakes.


Here acceleration is measured as Sen’s slope of the annual first-differenced STL trend. Positive values mean faster warming, slower cooling, or a transition toward warming.

title: Algorithms
text:
Seasonal-Trend decomposition using LOESS (Cleveland et al. 1990). Decomposes a time series into seasonal, trend, and remainder components.
Key parameters:

Period: seasonal cycle length (12 for monthly data)
nt: width of the trend LOESS window — controls smoothness. Larger nt → smoother trend, less short-term detail. Sensitivity to nt should always be tested.
ni / no: inner/outer iterations for robustness

Used in: warming-acceleration.

title: Algorithms
text:
Seasonal-Trend decomposition using LOESS (Cleveland et al. 1990). Decomposes a time series into seasonal, trend, and remainder components.
Key parameters:

Period: seasonal cycle length (12 for monthly data)
nt: width of the trend LOESS window — controls smoothness. Larger nt → smoother trend, less short-term detail. Sensitivity to nt should always be tested.
ni / no: inner/outer iterations for robustness

Used in: warming-acceleration.

title: Algorithms
text:
STARS
Sequential T-test Analysis of Regime Shifts (Rodionov 2004). Detects abrupt shifts in the mean of a time series.

Sequential: processes data in order, near-real-time capable
Two parameters: cut-off length \(l\) and significance level \(p\)
Uses a moving window to estimate local variance; robust to isolated outliers
Regime length is not necessarily \(l\); a new regime can start at any point after the shift

Originally designed for PDO regime shifts. Applied to lake surface temperature by Woolway et al. (2017).
Alternatives: Bai-Perron (multiple breakpoints, regression-based), Pettitt (single shift, rank-based), CUSUM (cumulative sum). All are sensitive to algorithm choice and parameterisation; STARS is favoured for its simplicity and climatological track record.
Paper notes: Rodionov (2004).
Used in: warming-acceleration.

title: Algorithms
text:
K-means clustering
Partitions \(n\) observations into \(K\) groups by minimising within-cluster sum of squared distances. Requires pre-specification of \(K\); sensitive to feature scaling.
Common workflow: z-score features → run K-means → order clusters by a meaningful axis (e.g. mean temperature).

title: Algorithms
text:
Theil–Sen slope
Non-parametric slope estimator: median of all pairwise slopes. Robust to outliers, no normality assumption. Widely used in climate trend analysis.

title: Algorithms
text:
Mann–Kendall test
Non-parametric trend test. Tests whether a monotonic trend exists. Often paired with Theil–Sen for trend magnitude. Sensitive to autocorrelation — pre-whitening may be needed.

title: Metrics
text: Methods, algorithms, indices, data sources, and terminology. Not tied to any specific exploration.

title: Metrics
text:
Algorithms
STL, STARS, K-means, Theil–Sen, Mann–Kendall, etc.

title: Metrics
text:
Indices
ENSO, PDO, AMO, NAO, AO — large-scale climate oscillation indices.

title: Metrics
text:
Data
Datasets, variables, and measurement conventions.

title: Metrics
text:
Terms
Definitions of recurring concepts (global warming hiatus, regime shift, etc.).

title: Terms
text:
The period roughly 1998–2012 during which the rate of global mean surface temperature increase slowed relative to the preceding and following decades. Attributed to a combination of ocean heat uptake, internal variability (PDO negative phase), volcanic aerosols, and incomplete radiative forcing accounting (Medhaug et al. 2017, Nature).
Not a pause in warming — total heat content continued to rise — but a slowdown in the rate of surface temperature increase.

title: Terms
text:
The period roughly 1998–2012 during which the rate of global mean surface temperature increase slowed relative to the preceding and following decades. Attributed to a combination of ocean heat uptake, internal variability (PDO negative phase), volcanic aerosols, and incomplete radiative forcing accounting (Medhaug et al. 2017, Nature).
Not a pause in warming — total heat content continued to rise — but a slowdown in the rate of surface temperature increase.

title: Terms
text:
Regime shift
An abrupt, persistent change in the statistical properties (typically the mean) of a time series. Detected by algorithms such as STARS. Not the same as a trend change; a regime shift implies a relatively sudden transition between two quasi-stable states.

title: Terms
text:
Warming
In the context of lake temperature: the total change in LSWT over a study period, typically estimated via Theil–Sen slope × period length.

title: Terms
text:
Acceleration
The rate of change of the warming trend. Positive acceleration means warming is speeding up; negative means warming is slowing (not cooling). Estimated via Sen slope of first-differenced STL trend.

title: Indices
text:
Large-scale climate oscillation indices, typically derived from sea surface temperature or atmospheric pressure fields.

title: Data
text: Lake Surface Water Temperature. Satellite-derived skin temperature of lake surfaces.

title: Data
text: Lake Surface Water Temperature. Satellite-derived skin temperature of lake surfaces.

title: Data
text:
GLAST
Global Lake Surface Temperature dataset. Daily LSWT for ~92,000 lakes globally, 1981–2020. Derived from satellite thermal infrared imagery.

title: Data
text:
ERA5-Land
Reanalysis dataset from ECMWF. Provides land surface variables (temperature, radiation, wind, evaporation, etc.) at ~9 km resolution, hourly. Useful as auxiliary climate drivers for lake temperature analysis.

title: Data
text:
HydroLAKES
Global database of lake polygons and attributes (area, depth, volume, shoreline length, etc.). Used for lake morphometry and spatial classification.

title: Regional
text:
We classify lakes by the shape of their 1981–2020 annual STL-trend response. The current canonical classification uses per-lake baseline anomalies relative to 1981–1990, PCA retaining 95% of the trajectory variance, and \(k\)-means clustering. The selected solution has five clusters (\(K = 5\)).
These clusters describe low-frequency lake-surface-temperature response types: lakes with similar STL-trend trajectories are grouped together regardless of continental labels.






NoteFinding 10



Five STL-trend response types are identified. The classes separate weak or plateauing responses from late-accelerating and sustained-warming trajectories; their spatial distributions provide an external check on whether the data-driven classification corresponds to coherent regional climate-response patterns.










Figure 1: STL-trend response clusters and their thermal metric distributions. (A) Global spatial distribution of the five clusters. (B-D) Violin plots of mean temperature, Sen slope, and STL-trend difference slope by cluster.

Figure 2: Two-dimensional density of warming speed and acceleration by STL-trend response cluster. Contour lines show per-cluster density; marginal panels show univariate density by cluster.








C1: Late-accelerating moderate warming. This cluster contains 20,444 lakes. The mean STL-trend anomaly rises slowly during the first half of the record and more strongly after 2000, reaching about +0.86 °C in 2020 relative to the 1981–1990 baseline.
C2: Early decline / late rebound. This cluster contains 12,072 lakes. The mean trajectory is flat to slightly negative through the 1990s, then turns upward after the early 2000s and reaches about +0.67 °C by 2020.
C3: Strong sustained warming. This is the smallest but strongest warming cluster, with 8,581 lakes. The mean STL-trend anomaly increases steadily across the full 40-year record and reaches about +1.15 °C in 2020.
C4: Weak / near-stable warming. This is the largest cluster, with 29,657 lakes. The mean response remains close to the baseline throughout the record, with only weak warming and a 2020 anomaly of about +0.15 °C.
C5: Early warming then plateau. This cluster contains 21,491 lakes. The mean trajectory rises through the 1990s and early 2000s, then flattens or slightly declines, ending near +0.31 °C in 2020.

Temp is mean STL trend in °C. Warming is Sen slope of annual STL trend in °C / 40 yr. Accel is Sen slope of first-differenced STL trend in 10-3 °C / yr2. Dominant is the continent with the highest fraction of lakes.

title: Regional
text:
We classify lakes by the shape of their 1981–2020 annual STL-trend response. The current canonical classification uses per-lake baseline anomalies relative to 1981–1990, PCA retaining 95% of the trajectory variance, and \(k\)-means clustering. The selected solution has five clusters (\(K = 5\)).
These clusters describe low-frequency lake-surface-temperature response types: lakes with similar STL-trend trajectories are grouped together regardless of continental labels.






NoteFinding 10



Five STL-trend response types are identified. The classes separate weak or plateauing responses from late-accelerating and sustained-warming trajectories; their spatial distributions provide an external check on whether the data-driven classification corresponds to coherent regional climate-response patterns.










Figure 1: STL-trend response clusters and their thermal metric distributions. (A) Global spatial distribution of the five clusters. (B-D) Violin plots of mean temperature, Sen slope, and STL-trend difference slope by cluster.








Cluster

Figure 2: Two-dimensional density of warming speed and acceleration by STL-trend response cluster. Contour lines show per-cluster density; marginal panels show univariate density by cluster.








C1: Late-accelerating moderate warming. This cluster contains 20,444 lakes. The mean STL-trend anomaly rises slowly during the first half of the record and more strongly after 2000, reaching about +0.86 °C in 2020 relative to the 1981–1990 baseline.
C2: Early decline / late rebound. This cluster contains 12,072 lakes. The mean trajectory is flat to slightly negative through the 1990s, then turns upward after the early 2000s and reaches about +0.67 °C by 2020.
C3: Strong sustained warming. This is the smallest but strongest warming cluster, with 8,581 lakes. The mean STL-trend anomaly increases steadily across the full 40-year record and reaches about +1.15 °C in 2020.
C4: Weak / near-stable warming. This is the largest cluster, with 29,657 lakes. The mean response remains close to the baseline throughout the record, with only weak warming and a 2020 anomaly of about +0.15 °C.
C5: Early warming then plateau. This cluster contains 21,491 lakes. The mean trajectory rises through the 1990s and early 2000s, then flattens or slightly declines, ending near +0.31 °C in 2020.

title: Regional
text:
Regime-shift timing by response type
Each cluster has its own characteristic regime-shift timing. If the clustering captures real differences in climate response, lakes within the same cluster should share similar shift-year patterns.






NoteFinding 12



Each cluster shows distinct shift-year peaks. The eight clusters differ not only in spatial pattern but also in physical characteristics. Temperature level, warming rate, acceleration, and regime-shift timing all vary systematically across clusters. STL trend shapes and shift timing are consistent within clusters and distinct between clusters.

title: Regional
text:
Spatial coherence
The eight clusters exhibit different spatial coherence patterns. Some form tight geographic clusters; others form latitudinal bands spanning multiple continents.






NoteFinding 13



Five clusters (C2, C3, C5, C6, C7) form tight geographic clusters, with more than 80% of lakes within ±30° of their centroid. Two clusters (C1, C8) form latitudinal bands spanning the full longitude range at high latitudes. One cluster (C4, tropical) is globally dispersed but concentrated in the subtropical latitude band.

Time range of GloboLakes and ARC-Lake is limited, not covering the post-hiatus period.
Use ESA CCI validate the post-hiatus trend.



 Back to top

Results is parameters-sensitive. E.g., min-segment, different method.
Considering turning to quantitative analysis: calculate the slope or something.



 Back to top

Rerun STL again. With nt=199, trend component is significantly smoothed, sawtooth basically eliminated.
And do the STARS regime shift detection on the trend component.



 Back to top

Discussed with advisor. Several points to deal.
Ice. In previous explore, I don’t even remember how I dealed with the 0 temperature data. Need to calculate the ice cover days in monthly and annual scale.
Clustering. Too many clusters, hard to explain the meaning of each cluster.
Attribution. Teleconnection analysis showed that the 5 teleconnection indices are not significantly correlated with the wamring speed and acceleration. Maybe we need to consider the local climate factors or geographical factors.



 Back to top

Feedback from advisor: progress is slow, prone to getting stuck in details; should quickly shift to a paper-writing
Adjust the story. Not using the “hiatus” as the lead, but first describe “what is observed”, and then cautiously discuss “what it might mean”.
So the main parts of the paper should contain: - Global scale description - Regional and temporal scale description - Driving factors (optional) - Ecological effects (optional)



 Back to top

Back from the “business trip”, relaunch the STL decomposition workflow (interrupted for nearly 3 weeks!).
Rerun STL with adjusted parameters, takes about 1 hour for one run.
TOCHECK: whether the sawtooth in the trend component of the new decomposition result is improved



 Back to top

After deal with the ice cover days, result changes a little, but not much.
Try to use 1981–1990 as the baseline period to normalize the time series. And then do PCA and clustering.
Should I use the raw series or the STL trend to do PCA and clustering?

Thoughts
Let’s figure all the things out.
Mean and trend can be calculated from the temperature time series. Mean means the average temperature level, and trend means the warming speed.
If we do a first-difference on it, we can get the overall acceleration by the trend of the 1st-difference series. E.g., the acceleration is i °C/year2, meaning that the warming speed increases i °C/year every year on average.
And, since we have the great STARS algorithm, we can also detect the regime shifts in the 1st-difference (hereinafter referred to as 1d) series. STARS detects the shifts in the mean of the series. Regimes differ in the mean. So if we apply STARS to the 1d series, we can detect the shifts in the warming speed, with each shifts representing a change in the warming speed.

Current
First of all, almost all lakes are warming, but among all lakes: - warming speed is different - acceleration is different
For one lake, the warming speed is not constant, it changes over time. So we can detect the shifts in the warming speed.
About the clustering, we now use the STL trend to compute the annual anomaly relative to a baseline period, which is 1981–1990. Then we do PCA and clustering on the anomaly series.
K-means clustring, K=5 is the best parameter.

I think it is needed to compare different algorithms and parameters.
Suggestions from advisor: detrending; regional grouping.



 Back to top

Finished 9 lake temperature / climate change literature notes.
Key points: Xiao and Ren (2023) (North Pacific SST 2013/14 regime shift), Pi et al. (2022) (GLAKES dataset), Cohen et al. (2014), Dai and Wang (2018), Tong et al. (2023), Woolway et al. (2017), Xie and Kosaka (2017)

Tong, Yan, Lian Feng, Xinchi Wang, Xuehui Pi, Wang Xu, and R. Iestyn Woolway. 2023. “Global Lakes Are Warming Slower Than Surface Air Temperature Due to Accelerated Evaporation.” Nature Water 1 (11): 929–40. https://doi.org/10.1038/s44221-023-00148-8.


Woolway, R. Iestyn, Martin T. Dokulil, Wlodzimierz Marszelewski, Martin Schmid, Damien Bouffard, and Christopher J. Merchant. 2017. “Warming of Central European Lakes and Their Response to the 1980s Climate Regime Shift.” Climatic Change 142 (3): 505–20. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10584-017-1966-4.

Congnitive update after 3 months of no record.
Temporal scale of “hiatus” should be defined at decadal scale (10-15 years), previously misused 4-5 year window.
We might need 3 layer to illustrate the regime shift: - Statistical layer: mean/slope mutation - Climate dynamics layer: PDO/NAO/… mode flip - Ecological mechanism layer: feedback change
Core question: Are global lakes entering a warming acceleration phase after 2012?
Use STL decomposition + STARS analysis: 99.6% of lakes show overall warming, mean 0.58 °C.
Shifts peak at 1990 (8.0%), not a boundary artifact.
Big difference among regions.
PDO lagged bimodal (0 month / 36 month); AMO dominant (R=0.52, 99.1% positive correlation)



 Back to top

Finished a draft.
Downloading GloboLakes, ARC-Lake and HydroLAKES data.

title: Explorations
text: All explorations — finished, cancelled, in-progress, or not started.

title: Explorations
text:
Warming Acceleration

prose · draft · log


Do global lakes exhibit heterogeneous warming acceleration — or deceleration — across space and time, and what drives it?
92,245 lakes, GLAST dataset, 1981–2020.
Methods: STL decomposition, STARS regime shifts, K-means clustering, teleconnection attribution.
The question began with the global warming hiatus as a starting point, but the focus has broadened to the full spatiotemporal structure of lake warming heterogeneity.

title: Hiatus Paper Collection
text: Collected sentences and excerpts from related papers, useful for writing the warming acceleration paper.

title: Hiatus Paper Collection
text:
Winslow et al. (2018) — ERL

155 lakes globally; 83% exhibited reduced warming rates during the hiatus
Two-period comparison (pre-hiatus vs hiatus) rather than changepoint detection
Lake-specific response heterogeneity not explained by morphometric attributes

title: Hiatus Paper Collection
text:
Zhou et al. (2023) — Nature Communications

“the rate of lake heating slows as air warms”
Evaporative cooling as negative feedback on lake warming
Local climate (air temp, wind, humidity, irradiance), land cover, geomorphology, and water transparency explain >30% of lake–air temperature difference variance
Clear, cold, deep, high-altitude, undisturbed lakes are most sensitive to air temperature changes

title: Hiatus Paper Collection
text:
O’Reilly et al. (2015) — GRL

Global lake summer SST warming at ~0.34 °C/decade
GLAST dataset: satellite-derived lake surface water temperature

title: Hiatus Paper Collection
text:
Woolway et al. (2017) — Climatic Change

Wind stilling → extended stratification → surface warming amplification
UK lake simulations

title: Hiatus Paper Collection
text:

Systematic documentation of long-term ice season shortening in N. hemisphere lakes

title: Hiatus Paper Collection

Multi-mechanism review of the global warming hiatus
Ocean heat uptake, internal variability, incomplete forcings

title: Hiatus Paper Collection

Best practices for climate changepoint detection
Sensitivity to assumptions; external validation needed for attribution

title: fyfe2016: Making Sense of the Early-2000s Warming Slowdown
text: NCC commentary, slowdown was real, but not a stop, and mainly driven by internal decadal variability plus natural forcing.
crumbs: fyfe2016: Making Sense of the Early-2000s Warming Slowdown

title: fyfe2016: Making Sense of the Early-2000s Warming Slowdown
text:
If there is a “hiatus”?
Some paper argues that the “hiatus” or “slowdown” is not real, lack of statistical significance, or just a bais in the data.
This paper says it’s real, statistically observable.
One important point is that we should pick the baseline period carefully. Some papers pick 1950-1999 and argue that the slowdown is not there. But the period that has the similar physical processes and human influence with the under review period (about 2001-2014) should be 1972-2001.
crumbs: fyfe2016: Making Sense of the Early-2000s Warming Slowdown

title: fyfe2016: Making Sense of the Early-2000s Warming Slowdown
text:
.
The vital scientific question is not “is there a hiatus/slowdown”, but “how do the internal variability and external forcing combine and influence the surface temperature trend”.
crumbs: fyfe2016: Making Sense of the Early-2000s Warming Slowdown

title: cohen2014: Recent Arctic Amplification and Extreme Mid-Latitude Weather
text:
Artic amplification.


citation_key: cohen2014 (Cohen et al. (2014))
title: Recent Arctic Amplification and Extreme Mid-Latitude Weather
author: Judah Cohen, James A. Screen, Jason C. Furtado, Mathew Barlow, David Whittleston, Dim Coumou, Jennifer Francis, Klaus Dethloff, Dara Entekhabi, James Overland, Justin Jones
journal: Nature Geoscience
year: 2014
doi: 10.1038/ngeo2234



The Arctic region has warmed more than twice as fast as the global average — a phenomenon known as Arctic amplification. The rapid Arctic warming has contributed to dramatic melting of Arctic sea ice and spring snow cover, at a pace greater than that simulated by climate models.





 Back to topReferences

Cohen, Judah, James A. Screen, Jason C. Furtado, et al. 2014. “Recent Arctic Amplification and Extreme Mid-Latitude Weather.” Nature Geoscience 7 (9): 627–37. https://doi.org/10.1038/ngeo2234.
crumbs: cohen2014: Recent Arctic Amplification and Extreme Mid-Latitude Weather

title: dai2018: Identifying the Early 2000s Hiatus Associated with Internal Climate Variability
text:
citation_key: dai2018 (Dai and Wang (2018))
title: Identifying the Early 2000s Hiatus Associated with Internal Climate Variability
author: Xin-Gang Dai, Ping Wang
journal: Scientific Reports
year: 2018
doi: 10.1038/s41598-018-31862-z



The hiatus is characterized as a near-zero trend on the decadal scale corresponding to the maximum P-value via an F-test in statistics.


The GWH is often attributed to internal climate variability, external forcing, or both. Recent cooling in the middle and eastern regions of the tropical Pacific has seemingly involved a phase change of the Interdecadal Pacific Oscillation (IPO) accompanying intensified trade winds. The GWH may also be associated with an increase in aerosols in the stratosphere during the period 2000–2010 because aerosols can increase optical depth, which generates countervailing forces against global warming. The GWH may also be explained in part by extensive heat uptake by the deep ocean or an extremely low number of sunspots during the latest solar activity cycle.


…, which indicates that infilling and bias correction in the datasets increase the temperature, especially during the early 2000s, probably due to rapid warming in the Arctic region. Cohen et al. (2014)




dai2018f1

Dai, Xin-Gang, and Ping Wang. 2018. “Identifying the Early 2000s Hiatus Associated with Internal Climate Variability.” Scientific Reports 8 (1): 13602. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-018-31862-z.
